sentences.text: In Nuremberg.
Well, Nuremberg-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nuremberg was rubble.
Nuremberg was completely disrupted.
It was the shell of a city, and our wonderful US Army superlatively managed most of this.
They took care of the logistics.
They brought all the food in, often powdered eggs and Spam and horrible army stuff.
But some-- and they hired all these people to come and manage the hotel and cook for us.
They had to organize everything.
The simplest things-- to get a car that would move and wasn't completely kaput, to make a telephone work, to telephone to all the outposts of Nuremberg, which were-- entire Europe was an outpost.
There was no place in the world, excuse me, at that time but Nuremberg.
Washington was watching Nuremberg.
England was watching Nuremberg.
There was no Paris, no Italy-- they were pulling up from the war.
And everybody who was anybody or who wasn't anybody was watching or in Nuremberg, or coming to visit Nuremberg, or writing about Nuremberg.

Walter Cronkite, later, Willy Brandt, many people who later were prime ministers and big British politicians, political figures, many famous Russians who later played a big role in Moscow were all in Nuremberg.
As a girl in OSS said, when she heard I was going to Nuremberg-- oh, I'd like to go there, too, she said.
Everybody who is anybody is in Nuremberg-- OSS's attitude, you see.
And it was just the place to be and to watch.
Reporters had to get an assignment in Nuremberg.
And the world watched because we had been-- we had been in the paws of this man, Hitler.
We still thought he might be alive at that time.
We had been in his-- in his hands, and now here were these people who supposedly had caused all this trouble.
Well, they talk about-- nobody can count it-- 50 million dead of the war dead.
And everybody was watching Nuremberg.
And Nuremberg was a mess.
And the heroic US Army really took care of everybody.
The first winter was very cold.
Somehow the coal supplies didn't come through, so everybody wore the best, biggest coats and sweaters they had.
And the little French girls who had been in the resistance-- the French men, too, I suppose, but I noticed the French girls-- they put on everything they had.
They were so cold with their resistance life-- came into Nuremberg.
And everybody pitched in.

There was a tremendous spirit of work, and the work was tremendously diversified because these endless documents were funneling into Nuremberg from-- from army cars all over Europe, were bringing in, from Paris, especially.
Hitler in the last days-- I always say Hitler, he didn't probably know what was going on-- had carried great loads of documents, truckloads of documents, out to Paris to store.
It was getting so bombed out in Berlin.

And there was a great spirit of cooperation, and in a sense, everybody, including, strangely enough, as they assemble them-- the defendants-- was in the German population-- was in some way so relieved that the war was over.
And they were all talkative.
They talked about their crimes.
They talked about Hitler, himself.
There was no effort.
It was just a people who had been pulled out of a shock situation, the war, and didn't know when it would end, and it had ended.
Oh, well, we were doing the translation, as I said.
I never certainly even tried out for the interpreting.

That was-- those were the people who really were very capable people, linguists and-- somehow I-- a different approach, a very wonderful alacrity in their interpretation and so forth, who could do that.
There were big tests, and they called Colonel Dostert 'Napoleon,' because he was so strict with them.
He was a small, rather heavy man-- I guess Napoleon, too, was that-- and he was very strict.
He was tough with all these people that lined up-- former professors-- to take the test, and see if they could become an interpreter.
That was something better.
I didn't even try for that.
But at any rate, contact with the defendants-- the first big trial, which took a little over a year, was the big, prominent trial-- 'Goering et al,' they called it.
And one day, they brought a translation and said, would I please translate it.
I don't know-- they wanted me to translate it.
And it was such an interesting thing.
It was about three lines, and it said in English, 'from now on, and also toward the outside.' I thought, how do you translate that?
My memory is functioning, and I thought-- that's Hess, Rudolf Hess.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] I remember the words.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] 'up.' [SPEAKING GERMAN].
My memory is at my service, more or less.
Now, how to translate [SPEAKING GERMAN] It was special Hess language, you know.
And also toward the outside.
My memory is functioning and also toward the outside.
As in, I'll talk now, you know?
And so I went back to the lady who used to be my supervisor in Washington.
I said, Herma, how do you translate that?
And she gave me her version.
I still didn't like that too well, anyhow.
I've learned a lot of German in the meantime, since I'd been in her employ.
And anyway, I translated it and took it to the higher-ups, and they took it still higher up.
Nobody said it was Rudolf Hess, but it was pretty clear that it was Rudolf Hess.
And the next day, it was read in the trial.
So that was just like a lottery and this interesting little tidbit was brought to me.
I don't know why, but anyway, that's the way it is.
And contact with the defendants-- I did later in the subsequent proceedings.
Those were the 12 trials, also called the Nuremberg Trials, that took place after the Goering trial ended in October 1946, and lasted about three more years.
And there, I worked for Robert Kempner, and he had also been in a big trial.
I had only known him as a man, well-known man, that was down at the table, usually with a large group of women, and sometimes with eight or nine girls.
He didn't mind if they were good-looking, he didn't mind if they were women.
His mother had been a woman's actionist, a early fighter for women's rights.
And I'd seen him at that table.
He was kind of conspicuous because he always had eight or nine women and Robert Kempner, you know?
And took them all on.
And later, I went to work for him, let's say at the beginning of 1947.
And with him, I went to his interrogations.

He did a great many interrogations going to the prison, and the defendants or the witnesses that were under arrest would be brought to a interrogation room, and he would interrogate with a secretary present, with me.
And you will be interested-- I think a rumor went the rounds that I was a psychologist.
And as I said nothing, I puzzled them, you know.
The type-- the secretary or the stenographer was-- there were sometimes two or three stenographers-- were busy, and they couldn't quite figure me out.
Once in a while, he would ask me something quietly in English, and I would say something to him or I would say something to him.
And I think the rumor had gone around that I was a psychologist.
They frighten people.
I don't know whether you know that.
[LAUGHS]
It was very funny because I didn't realize this, and I wasn't a psychologist.
[LAUGHS] Who's afraid of a Latin teacher?

But anyway, we went-- I had-- you talk about contact with the defendants-- with these defendants of the post-- the big trial and then the 12 succeeding proceedings that were before the US court, a military court-- I had almost constant contact with the defendants, because he was going to the prison many times a week, sometimes with a guest that would be interested or sometimes with someone else that he thought could confront the defendant with something shocking to stir them up, to make them tell it as it was, to get the story of these different people.
And he was a man of great courage.
As the documents told it, he made the indictments.
You couldn't-- you couldn't quibble with him.
And many people would even come to him and say, Dr.
Kempner, you don't realize what you're doing.
That will cause anti-Semitism.
And he'd say, well, what can I do?
The evidence says these men were responsible.
Look at this document, he would say.
You know?
And so he was a most courageous person.
He felt that whoever was responsible for this should at least be brought before the court.
And Nuremberg service, as we gradually realized, was not just to put people to punishment.
He calls it in his memoirs a 'laboratory.' It brought all these things to the surface that otherwise would have rotted without attention.
How it happened was his puzzle.
How could it happen?
These were well-educated persons.
These were people of the privileged class.
I mean, a desperado that does something for money or-- or has-- you know, there are other-- other aspects to his crime.
You can understand why he did it.
You wonder, would I have done it?
Would I have been driven to it?
But these were people of the privileged classes, very often.
And he puzzled over them and tried to get out of them why, what was their-- well, of course, Hitler had a great tool in his hand.
As soon as he got the war going, people throughout the world have this feeling, I have to be patriotic, my country is in a state of war.
So there were, shall we say, temporizing or ameliorating circumstances behind some of their thinking and so forth.
But contact with the defendants?
Yes, you would see how they answered, you know.

And as he said in his memoirs, they didn't talk any different from the little criminals that I used to have when I was in my 20s as a trial lawyer in Berlin, you know-- the simple criminals that, you know, that--
And he said, these were the mass murderers in a way, the so-called 'desktop murderers.' And their explanation of why they did it was more or less the same sort of argumentation, he felt.
That was his analysis of it.
Oh, often.
But it was a privilege to go to the courtroom.
You had to get-- well, with Dr.
Kempner in his own trial, which was a very long trial, I would go with him always.
Almost always-- sometimes he took one or two other people.
But almost always, I would go with him because I had done this research with him, and I had done the documentation with him.
And I would sit there beside him as a helper.
Sometimes there would be another helper on his left-hand side, and we would help him in the trial-- go get this for me or I forgot that, can you remember or something like that.
So [INAUDIBLE] was in the trial very often, very often.
And the later, very noble President Richard [NON-ENGLISH], the son of one of our defendants, was a few meters away from me.
And he's very kind, always very kind to Robert Kempner.
He understood this whole thing-- he had been a soldier, and he understood this and saw all these documents and realized what incrimination in their very often tragic and weak careers.
They knew better, they didn't want to do this.
Shall I let my job go?
Shall I let Hitler get another Nazi into this?
They all thought of themselves as anti-Nazis, you know.
So the courtroom was a very interesting place.
Our trial, the one I worked mostly on, had, I think, 21 or 22 defendants.
They were all ministerial-level government officials, and a few of them were admitted Nazi enthusiasts.
And interesting-- their reactions and everything.
The sentences were very small in these trials, largely very small.
And unfortunately, as the Cold War developed, the German protests to protect them caused a lot of commutations or simpling of sentences.
But Dr.
Kempner was never discouraged about this.
He felt-- he called it the-- what did he call it-- the-- the pity, the [NON-ENGLISH].
Everybody was calling for pity for these poor men, you know?
And they didn't spend very long-- some of them died.
It was a very harsh thing for them, psychologically, in the world press and so forth.

And he always felt that Nuremberg's contribution to society was that it showed the documentation of this thing, and it was a warning that crime is crime, even if big government employees manipulate it.
And he felt that was Nuremberg's prime contribution to law, you know.
Recently, I read the most horrible thing.
Dr.
Kempner has one or two volumes in my apartment, and I was going through them.

And he had marked-- he prepared me for this-- he had marked-- I should have read it before I came-- he had marked the pages where some of these very frank witnesses, true believers in Nazism and anti-Semitism and the Holocaust-- in the real sense, believers that it should be done, that it was a great deed, the Holocaust-- told how when they ran out-- This man told of his-- I don't even remember his name, I'm sorry to say.
I could supply you with it.
It's in this volume.
There are many volumes of the records of the Nuremberg Trials, you know, of subsequent proceedings.
And there it showed how when they ran out of their Zyklon-B gas, or whatever it was, they had to kill so-and-so many people.
There were so-and-so many there.
They were coming in every day.
They were under duress.
And they were dutiful.
And they smashed the babies against the wall to kill them quick.
Now, that was to me the most horrible thing.
They smashed the babies with their-- with their arms.
These are not our defendants, who were refined men, and I'm sure wouldn't have done that.
But our refined men were behind these people, you know what I mean?
And this was real brutality, but it didn't seem to affect them.
That was their duty-- they had run out of their other munitions, and they had to do their quota.
A baby couldn't do any work building a road or anything, you know.
That, I found the most shocking thing I ever saw.
That was testimony in the trial.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We found a very interesting document in the course of this trial called the 'Endlosung.' That means the final solution.
And that was a group of what we Washingtonians-- I guess the Germans probably said the same word for it-- interdepartmental meeting.

The Justice Department, the Foreign Office-- about 14 of these interdepartmental officials, their top dogs-- went to a meeting under the auspices of the SS on January 20, 1942, and had a meeting so that Heydrich, who was given by Goering-- Goering was not innocent but didn't do any of this, gave it to Heydrich to do.
Heydrich called this meeting, and all of the officials, the top dogs-- maybe there were, I don't know how many people were there.
Were there 20 people there?
The document is available.
And they-- he told them how it was going to be, that they were going to gather all the Jews from throughout Europe, comb through Europe.
It's a well-known document by now.
It's 15 pages long, and I translated it originally.
Dr.
Kempner wanted it quickly, and I translated it.
And it was hard to translate.
It was very rough German.
It was strange German.
But it told its story.
You could see what they projected.
And he-- that was the minutes of this meeting, which lasted, I suppose, a couple of hours.
And then they had a nice whisky, in a lovely villa, in a lovely lake in Berlin area.
We went there afterwards-- it's now a Holocaust Memorial, I believe-- a-- one of many.

And they-- he told them, all of these people that you think would find it horrifying, you know, 18-20 people, to go back and tell their agencies-- this is the minutes of the meeting, 15 pages long-- how it was going to be done.
The people were going to be brought over to-- to the east.
They thought it was better outside Germany.
And there they were to be brought to camps.

And those that were-- as they arrived, since they would be hard to feed them and so forth-- though they should be sorted out by very good physicians which ones could still do some good work, and quickly sorted.
And the others right away-- they just disburdened themselves of the others, you know.
And that was almost unbelievable.
And these minutes were distributed in 47 different copies to all the different agencies for their records in their different regions.
And we found one copy-- one of the girls who will come to this reunion-- an English girl at that time.
She married an American and lives near Philadelphia now-- Betty Richardson-- I guess-- I don't know what her married name is Nuk-- I think it might be N-U-K.
But she's here.
You could even find her and see how it was to find that document.
She was in Berlin, the outpost Berlin, going through some of the agency files-- the German agency files-- and she found this.
And she called up and said, this is very startling.
This was in spring 1947.
The Goering trial was all gone.
And here comes the plan, the master plan-- how it was planned out and how it was distributed so all the agencies would know it.
And after they left the meeting, the [NON-ENGLISH], Eichmann and Heydrich-- Heydrich had to go off to Prague.
He was in charge of Prague at that moment before he, himself, was murdered.
And after the meeting, they had a little whiskey by the fireside.
It's a beautiful villa in Berlin.
If you go to Berlin, you should visit it.
And he said, well, now we have-- even-- even the popes of the-- of the civil service are backing us.
Because there was almost no protest in this meeting.
But there were a few protests.
And funny reasons-- that might have been to kind of call the crazy dogs off, you know?
One man said, well, wouldn't it be better to wait until the war.
They had different-- three or four of them did make a very mild effort to protest.
But after the war when Dr.
Kempner was interrogating these people in Nuremberg, he asked all these people if they'd been at that meeting.
No, no, I never had anything to do with it.
And he said, well, what about this?
And he said, I don't know why you don't say you were there.
You were one of the very few who spoke up against it.
But they didn't want to have anything to do with it, you know.
And they didn't know it was so beautifully documented.
And, of course, Eichmann and Heydrich had a very good reason for documenting it.
We didn't do it-- this whole group of the government did it.
You see what I mean?
On the other hand, there was a kind of boasting in it for the SS, too-- even for the government servants.
They thought, well, after the war is over, Hitler will start giving out prizes and better jobs.
I want to have him see what we did for his cause, you know.
So it was true government or, let's say, human career effort, you know, to show that you had played your part.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, it's sad because many of them were not that type at all.

You just feel without Hitler, they would have been good employees in some other-- [LAUGHS] But it shows how weak we human beings are, if you're working for the mighty dollar or the mighty mark, or something.
[LAUGHS]
How did I come to work for Dr.
Kempner?
Well, everyone knew Dr.
Kempner, and Nuremberg as-- whatever you want to call it-- an establishment, was in a constant state of flux.
A trial would end, another would start.
Four or five groups translating sections would be disbanded, and the one or two that were left would go to another translating group.
And, I don't know.
He had an opening.
I went around, talked to him, and he said, fine fine, come in Monday, or something like that.
You know?
We moved-- we were kind of mobile.
We moved somehow from job to job, and I had the great good fortune of moving into something that turned out to be very, very critical and interesting.
Dr.
Kempner-- his life was Nuremberg.
And I have the good fortune that a book, a short book, an excerpt from his is his German memoirs.
And these were a series of interviews, you might say, with the questions taken out about his life-- which was a very interesting life.

And I took a section of a little over 100 pages, and have the good fortune to have an English book coming out, a little book-- not intended for sale, but for the family and maybe for some of the prosecutors that knew him, and don't have his German language book because we are in America, after all.
And in his book, he tells what Nuremberg meant to him.
And if you look at it, he lived Nuremberg.
Before the-- we heard of Nuremberg, before Hitler came to power, he was fighting Nazism.
Nazism didn't just come from today to tomorrow, but developed, got its grip on different localities, different voting areas.
And he was fighting-- at that time, he was in Berlin in the employ of the Interior Ministry and the Justice Ministry of the pre-Hitler government, the Weimar Republic.
And he failed in his effort to get Hitler prosecuted and kicked out of the country as a dangerous foreigner.
Hitler was an Austrian to begin with, you know?
And he failed in that.

And as Hitler came in, he's riding along with his dear mother, the bacteriologist-- she rode in with him to work to Berlin every day-- and starts crying as she sees all the swastikas and says, now the pogroms will come to us.
His mother felt it coming.
And it was terribly hard for her.
Her son, her other son, had already gone to America.
He was a physician.
And he had all of these German employees.

Hitler fired them the day he got in, more or less, and they couldn't work any longer as doctors in government employ, as lawyers, as judges-- or private lawyers even, excepting they took a-- would take on a Jewish case.
They were still allowed to do that, but I mean, that's a tiny minority in Germany, and they're trying to get out as fast as they can.
But he-- as Hitler came in, he joined with a former judge who had lost his job.
And they made a kind of advisory office for Jews who wanted to get out of Germany to try to show them the ropes, to study the ropes themselves.
Where can I take a little money out?
There were different regulations if you were leaving the country.
And he and his partner, now dead, [NON-ENGLISH], for two years, did this.
That was allowed.
They were doing a service for Jewish people.
And in March 12, 1935, they're getting along fairly well.
Hitler is all over the place, getting worse every day, but they're getting their people out.
And on March 12, the gestapo came early in the morning, and took him away from his home in the suburbs of Berlin.
And they took him to a horrible camp, or-- or SS torture palace in Berlin.
And he was reported dead in the Dutch newspapers.
His name had been found in a journalist that they had declared war on.
The journalist was living in France.
And in the diary, they had kidnapped this journalist, who had visited Basel, and been kidnapped across the border into Germany.
And looking into his [NON-ENGLISH] book, his little diary, they saw Robert Kempner.
And his partner, also.
And they arrested them immediately.
And Dr.
Kempner didn't know what the charge was against him, you know.
Do you know a certain [NON-ENGLISH]?
That was this German journalist that they had gotten the notebook or the thing out.
And this got to be an international dispute between Switzerland-- praise heaven there's a Switzerland-- between Switzerland and the German government.
And Hitler said, that's too hot to hold.
Let those men out of prison.
Because Switzerland was going to go to the international governments and appeal this case.
How could a person be kidnapped down the streets of Basel and taken to Germany and put in--
So he-- they wanted the whole thing quashed-- no trial.
Switzerland brought so much pressure to bear on the German government that they quashed the whole thing.
So they let Dr.
Kempner out, but he had no more appetite to stay out in business.
And very shortly after that, he went to Florence, Italy.
He went to Florence, Italy, in order to have a school there for German children that Jewish children that couldn't go to German school.
And that was a period of his life there.
